title: Zechariah 5
subtitle: The Flying Scroll and the Woman in a Basket

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — "The Sixth Vision: The Flying Scroll"

heb.paragraph: The image of a megillah (scroll) in flight combines two domains: the written word of divine law and the dynamic movement of curse-judgment sweeping across the land. The scroll's dimensions — twenty cubits by ten cubits (approximately 30 x 15 feet) — match those of the tabernacle's Holy Place (Exod 26:15-25) and Solomon's temple porch (1 Kgs 6:3), suggesting that the curse emanates from the sanctuary itself. The two representative sins — theft and false oath — correspond to violations of the second table (human relations) and the first table (divine relations) of the Decalogue respectively.

refs.note: The curses of Deuteronomy's covenant renewal ceremony provide the legal background for the flying scroll's curse: covenant violation triggers automatic sanctions embedded in the covenant itself.
Ezekiel's scroll 'written on both sides' with 'words of lament and mourning and woe' parallels Zechariah's scroll written on both sides with judgments against thieves and perjurers.
Malachi's warning that God will come near 'for judgment' against 'those who swear falsely' continues the same post-exilic concern for covenant integrity that the flying scroll addresses.

notes.note: The scroll's dimensions matching the temple porch are theologically significant: judgment originates from the holy place, from God's presence. The curse is not arbitrary punishment but covenantal accountability. The community that enjoys God's restored presence must live according to His standards.

Theft and false oaths represent the social and religious dimensions of covenant violation respectively. A community that steals from one another and lies under oath before God cannot sustain the restored worship that the visions have promised. The curse 'entering the house and remaining there' indicates a judgment that is not instantaneous but gradual — persistent sin leads to structural collapse.

Calvin read the flying scroll as a reminder that God's grace in restoration does not eliminate His justice in judgment. The post-exilic community was tempted to assume that the end of exile meant the end of accountability. The scroll refutes this presumption: the covenant's blessings and curses are inseparable. Where God dwells, holiness is required.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The dimensions of the scroll (20 x 10 cubits) have been linked to the porch of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:3), suggesting that the curse proceeds from the sanctuary. The NET notes observe that such enormous dimensions indicate a vision of supernatural proportions rather than a physical document.
The word alah means both 'curse' and 'oath,' creating a double meaning: the scroll embodies the oath-curse of the covenant — the automatic sanction triggered by covenant violation.

boda.source: Mark J. Boda, Haggai / Zechariah, NIVAC — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Boda reads the flying scroll as the 'covenant enforcement mechanism' of the restored community. The visions have promised restoration, empowerment, and messianic hope (visions 1-5), but the scroll reminds the community that covenant blessing is inseparable from covenant obedience. The two sins — theft and perjury — represent what Boda calls 'horizontal' and 'vertical' covenant violation: sins against neighbor and sins against God. The curse's self-executing nature (it 'enters' and 'destroys' without human judicial intervention) signals that ultimate justice belongs to God, not to human courts.

hist.context: The sixth vision shifts from the positive themes of restoration and empowerment (visions 1-5) to the negative: the purging of sin from the restored community. The flying scroll is a curse (alah) that moves across the entire land, targeting two specific sins: stealing and swearing falsely. These sins are not random selections but represent comprehensive covenant violation — crimes against neighbor (theft) and crimes against God (perjury). The scroll enters the houses of offenders and 'remains in their house and destroys it' (5:4), indicating that the curse is not merely punitive but corrosive: unaddressed sin will consume the community from within. This vision balances the gracious restoration of visions 1-5 with the covenantal demand for holiness: the rebuilt community must be a purified community.

sections.header: Verses 5–11 — "The Seventh Vision: The Woman in a Basket"

heb.paragraph: The ephah is a dry measure of approximately 22 liters (about 5 gallons), used for measuring grain (Ruth 2:17; 1 Sam 1:24). In Zechariah 5:6-8, the ephah functions as a container large enough to hold a seated woman — obviously a visionary exaggeration emphasizing the supernatural character of the scene. The ephah may symbolize commerce and economic life, suggesting that the 'wickedness' contained within it is particularly associated with commercial dishonesty (cf. Amos 8:5; Mic 6:10).

The identification of the seated woman as rishʼah ('wickedness') personifies sin as a female figure — not because women are more sinful, but because 'wickedness' is a feminine noun in Hebrew and personification follows grammatical gender. The lead cover pressed down over her represents the containment and suppression of wickedness: God is not merely punishing sin but removing it from the community and exporting it to the land of its origin (Babylonia).

refs.note: Shinar is the land where Babel was built (Gen 11:2), making it the prototypical location of human rebellion against God — the fitting destination for personified wickedness.
John's 'Babylon the Great, the mother of prostitutes and of the abominations of the earth' develops Zechariah's personification of wickedness, scaling it to apocalyptic proportions.
The scapegoat that carries Israel's sins into the wilderness provides a ritual parallel: sin is removed from the community by being physically transported elsewhere.

notes.note: The vision of wickedness sealed in a basket is a graphic demonstration of God's power to contain and remove evil. The lead cover pressed down on the woman indicates that this is not a voluntary departure but a forced removal. God does not negotiate with wickedness; He suppresses and exports it.

The two women with stork wings are agents of removal, not objects of worship. Their stork-like wings may connote uncleanness (the stork is listed among unclean birds in Lev 11:19), suggesting that the agents who carry wickedness are themselves associated with the impure sphere. The destination — Shinar/Babylon — is the symbolic homeland of anti-God rebellion, where wickedness will be given its own 'house' or temple, far from the holy land.

Calvin interpreted the removal of wickedness to Shinar as God's definitive act of purification for the restored community. The vision assures believers that sin will not have permanent dominion in God's territory. Calvin noted the irony: Babylon, which once imprisoned God's people, now becomes the prison for their sin. The reversal is complete — what enslaved Israel is itself enslaved.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes observe that the ephah is 'the iniquity of the people throughout the land' — reading awon ('iniquity') instead of the MT's eyn ('appearance, eye'). This textual variant, supported by the LXX and Syriac, makes better sense in context and is adopted by most modern translations.

The phrase 'build a house for it' uses bayit ('house'), which can mean 'temple.' The construction of a shrine for wickedness in Shinar/Babylon parodies the temple-building theme central to Zechariah: as YHWH's house is built in Jerusalem, wickedness gets its own 'temple' in the land of rebellion.

boda.source: Mark J. Boda, Haggai / Zechariah, NIVAC — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Boda identifies the seventh vision as the 'purification by removal' counterpart to the sixth vision's 'purification by judgment.' Together, visions six and seven address the problem of sin from complementary angles: individual accountability (the scroll's curse) and corporate cleansing (the basket's export). The destination of Shinar/Babylon creates what Boda calls a 'reverse exile narrative': as Israel was once deported to Babylon, now it is wickedness that is deported from Israel. The lead cover and the construction of a permanent 'house' for wickedness indicate that this removal is final and irreversible within the visionary framework.

hist.context: The seventh vision complements the sixth by addressing the removal of wickedness from the land. Where the flying scroll threatened punishment for individual sinners, the woman in the basket depicts the corporate removal of wickedness itself — personified as a female figure sealed in an ephah with a lead cover. Two women with stork-like wings carry the basket to 'the country of Babylonia' (Shinar), where a 'house' (temple?) will be built for it. The image is one of reverse exile: as Israel was once carried to Babylon as punishment, now wickedness itself is exported back to its symbolic homeland. The vision assures the post-exilic community that the land of Israel is being purged of the evil that caused the exile, and that wickedness will be contained in its proper place — outside the covenant community.

themes.note: Chapter 5 is dominated by Judgment (the flying scroll's curse and the removal of wickedness), Holiness (the purification of the land from sin), Justice (covenant enforcement against theft and perjury), and Sovereignty (God's power to contain and export evil).

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: Sixth night vision: the flying scroll as covenant curse sweeping the land against thieves and perjurers

rows.label: vv.5-11
rows.text: Seventh night vision: personified wickedness sealed in an ephah and transported to Babylon for permanent containment

lit.note: Chapter 5 pairs two purification visions — judgment of individual sin (scroll) and removal of corporate wickedness (basket) — balancing the restoration promises of chapters 1-4 with the demand for covenant holiness.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Flying Scroll and the Woman in a Basket

translations.text: He asked me, “What do you see?” I answered, “I see a flying scroll, twenty cubits long and ten cubits wide.”
And he said to me, “What do you see?” I answered, “I see a flying scroll. Its length is twenty cubits, and its width ten cubits.”

The Lord Almighty declares, ‘I will send it out, and it will enter the house of the thief and the house of anyone who swears falsely by my name. It will remain in that house and destroy it completely, both its timbers and its stones.’ ”

I will send it out, declares the LORD of hosts, and it shall enter the house of the thief, and the house of him who swears falsely by my name. And it shall remain in his house and consume it, both timber and stones.”

thread.anchor: The Flying Scroll and the Woman in a Basket
thread.text: The scapegoat that carries Israel's sins into the wilderness provides a ritual parallel: sin is removed from the community by being physically transported elsewhere.

coaching.tip: A flying scroll (30 by 15 feet!) carries curses against thieves and perjurers. The scroll enters houses and 'consumes it, both timber and stones' (v. 4). God's word has forensic power — it executes judgment on violators. The Torah isn't passive text but active agent. Covenant words carry covenant consequences.

A woman named 'Wickedness' is stuffed into a basket and carried to Babylon (Shinar). Evil is being exported — removed from the land and housed where it belongs. Babylon as the reservoir of sin inverts the exile: Israel was sent to Babylon; now wickedness is sent there. The land is being cleansed of what contaminated it.
